questions.question: What is the main target cell of the TSST-1 toxin in toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: The TSST-1 toxin acts as a superantigen that binds directly to the TCR and MHC class II, massively activating CD4+ T lymphocytes without prior antigenic processing.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism triggers the TSST-1 toxin in toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: TSST-1 activates a large number of T lymphocytes, releasing a storm of cytokines (IL-2, TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma) that causes fever, hypotension and multiple organ failure.

questions.question: What is the first-line antimicrobial treatment in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Vancomycin covers MRSA and clindamycin inhibits toxin synthesis, being the combination of choice in severe staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome.

questions.question: Which clinical finding is characteristic of staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Diffuse maculopapular rash, followed by delayed desquamation, is a classic sign of toxic shock syndrome, along with high fever and hypotension.

questions.question: What is the main diagnostic criterion to define staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: The triad of fever ≥38.9°C, systolic hypotension ≤90 mmHg and diffuse rash constitutes the diagnostic core of staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome.

questions.question: What source of infection is most frequently associated with MRSA toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Surgical or post-traumatic wounds infected by MRSA are the most common focus, especially when occlusive dressings that promote toxin production are used.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of staphylococcal enterotoxins in food poisoning?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal enterotoxins act as superantigens that massively stimulate T lymphocytes, releasing cytokines that cause vomiting and diarrhea, without direct bacterial invasion.

questions.question: What is the typical incubation period for staphylococcal enterotoxin food poisoning?
questions.explanation: El período de incubación es corto (1-6 horas) porque la enterotoxina preformada actúa rápidamente, diferenciándose de infecciones bacterianas con períodos más largos.

questions.question: What type of toxin produced by Staphylococcus aureus causes toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Toxic shock syndrome toxin-1 (TSST-1) is a staphylococcal enterotoxin that acts as a superantigen, causing fever, hypotension, and multiple organ failure.

questions.question: Which of the following foods is most frequently associated with staphylococcal enterotoxin poisoning?
options: Processed meats and salads with mayonnaise
questions.explanation: Processed meats and salads with mayonnaise are common vehicles, since S. aureus grows in protein-rich foods and the enterotoxin resists heat.

questions.question: What characteristic differentiates staphylococcal enterotoxins from other dietary bacterial toxins?
options: They are inactivated by gastric pH
They are resistant to heat and freezing
They require live bacteria to act
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal enterotoxins are thermostable and resist freezing, allowing them to remain active even after cooking or refrigerating foods.

questions.question: What is the main treatment for staphylococcal enterotoxin food poisoning?
questions.explanation: Treatment is supportive with fluid and electrolyte replacement, since the disease is self-limiting and antibiotics do not act on preformed toxins.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of Staphylococcus aureus exfoliatins A and B?
questions.explanation: Exfoliatins A and B are serine proteases that degrade desmoglein 1, a component of desmosomes, causing the cells of the granular layer of the epidermis to separate, causing scalded skin syndrome.

questions.question: Which group of patients is most frequently affected by staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome predominantly affects neonates and children under 5 years of age, due to the immaturity of their kidney function to eliminate toxins and the lack of neutralizing antibodies.

questions.question: What is the main histological finding in staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome?
questions.explanation: The characteristic histological finding is an intraepidermal blister at the level of the granular layer, with a separation just below the stratum corneum, due to the action of exfoliatins on desmoglein 1.

questions.question: What type of toxin do the strains of Staphylococcus aureus that cause scalded skin syndrome produce?
questions.explanation: The strains responsible for scalded skin syndrome produce exfoliatins (A or B), which are specific serine proteases that cleave desmoglein 1, causing widespread peeling of the skin.

questions.question: What is the first-line antimicrobial treatment for staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome?
questions.explanation: Since most exfoliatin-producing strains are resistant to penicillin (MRSA), intravenous vancomycin is the drug of choice to cover methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in this syndrome.

questions.question: Which laboratory test helps differentiate staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome from toxic epidermal necrolysis?
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy with Gram stain allows identification of the level of epidermal separation: in scalded skin syndrome it is subcorneal intraepidermal, while in toxic epidermal necrolysis it is at the level of the dermoepidermal junction.

questions.question: Which Staphylococcus aureus toxin is typically associated with necrotizing pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL) is a pore-forming toxin produced by certain strains of Staphylococcus aureus, strongly associated with severe necrotizing pneumonia in previously healthy young patients.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of Panton-Valentine leukocidin?
questions.explanation: PVL is a two-component toxin that forms pores in the membrane of neutrophils and macrophages, causing cell lysis, cytokine release, and lung tissue necrosis.

questions.question: Which population is most susceptible to PVL necrotizing pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Necrotizing pneumonia due to PVL-producing strains predominantly affects children and young adults without comorbidities, with a fulminant clinical course and high mortality.

questions.question: What is the characteristic imaging finding of PVL pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Necrotizing pneumonia due to PVL manifests with pneumatoceles, cavitations and rapid destruction of the lung parenchyma on chest x-ray or CT.

questions.question: Which antibiotic should be avoided in suspected PVL-producing MRSA pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Vancomycin has poor pulmonary penetration and slow bacteriostatic activity, so linezolid or ceftaroline is preferred in PVL-MRSA necrotizing pneumonia.

questions.question: Which of the following strains of Staphylococcus aureus usually carries the lukS-PV/lukF-PV gene?
questions.explanation: The gene encoding PVL (lukS-PV/lukF-PV) is characteristic of community MRSA strains, associated with necrotizing infections in healthy individuals.

questions.question: What protein is encoded by the mecA gene in Staphylococcus aureus?
questions.explanation: The mecA gene encodes penicillin-binding protein 2a (PBP2a), a transpeptidase with low affinity for beta-lactams, responsible for methicillin resistance in S. aureus.

questions.question: What is the most common location of the mecA gene in MRSA?
questions.explanation: The mecA gene is located in a mobile genetic element called staphylococcal chromosome cassette (SCCmec), integrated into the bacterial chromosome, not in plasmids or phages.

questions.question: What resistance mechanism does PBP2a confer in MRSA?
questions.explanation: PBP2a is a modified penicillin-binding protein with low affinity for beta-lactams, which constitutes resistance by target modification, without inactivating the drug or pumping it.

questions.question: Which beta-lactam antibiotic maintains activity against MRSA due to its binding to PBP2a?
questions.explanation: Ceftaroline is a fifth-generation cephalosporin designed to bind with high affinity to PBP2a, overcoming mecA-mediated resistance, unlike other beta-lactams such as methicillin or amoxicillin.

questions.question: Which of the following genes is co-regulated with mecA in the resistance operon?
questions.explanation: mecI is the repressor gene of the mec operon, located upstream of mecA; together with mecR1 regulates the expression of PBP2a. blaZ encodes beta-lactamase, but is not part of the same operon.

questions.question: What type of resistance does the mecA gene confer to Staphylococcus aureus?
questions.explanation: The mecA gene encodes PBP2a, which confers resistance to all beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems) except ceftaroline and ceftobiprole, by reducing the affinity for these antibiotics.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics is typical of community-based MRSA?
questions.explanation: Community-based MRSA usually carries Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL), a toxin that causes tissue necrosis and abscesses, unlike nosocomial MRSA, which is multidrug-resistant.

questions.question: What types of infections does community-acquired MRSA most often cause?
questions.explanation: Community-based MRSA typically causes skin and soft tissue infections such as boils and abscesses, while nosocomial MRSA is more associated with invasive infections in hospitalized patients.

questions.question: What is the antibiotic resistance profile of nosocomial MRSA?
options: Resistant to beta-lactams and other groups
questions.explanation: Nosocomial MRSA is usually multidrug-resistant, with resistance to beta-lactams and frequently to other antibiotics such as macrolides, aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones, unlike community-acquired MRSA, which maintains sensitivity to more agents.

questions.question: What virulence factor is most characteristic of nosocomial MRSA?
questions.explanation: The most characteristic factor of nosocomial MRSA is methicillin resistance mediated by the mecA gene, which encodes a low-affinity PBP2a. Although it is not a toxin, it is its hallmark compared to community-based MRSA.

questions.question: What antibiotic is of choice for severe community-acquired MRSA infections?
questions.explanation: Clindamycin is an effective option for susceptible community MRSA infections, while vancomycin is reserved for clindamycin-resistant strains or nosocomial infections; Cephalexin and amoxicillin-clavulanate do not cover MRSA.

questions.question: What is the main reservoir of community MRSA in the population?
questions.explanation: The main reservoir of community MRSA is healthy nasal carriers, especially in children and young adults, unlike the nosocomial reservoir that is more associated with hospitalized patients and health personnel.

questions.question: What is the main function of coagulase in Staphylococcus aureus?
questions.explanation: Coagulase produces the conversion of fibrinogen into fibrin, forming a clot that protects the bacteria from phagocytosis and the immune response.

questions.question: What mechanism does Staphylococcus aureus protein A use to evade the immune response?
options: Binds to the Fc fragment of IgG
questions.explanation: Protein A binds to the Fc fragment of immunoglobulins G, orienting the Fab portion outward, which prevents opsonization and effective phagocytosis.

questions.question: Which of the following virulence factors is characteristic of Staphylococcus aureus and is used for its identification in the laboratory?
questions.explanation: Coagulase is a key enzyme that differentiates Staphylococcus aureus from other species of coagulase-negative staphylococci, being a fundamental diagnostic test.

questions.question: What effect does protein A have on complement activation?
questions.explanation: Protein A, by binding to the Fc of IgG, can activate the classical complement pathway in a non-specific manner, consuming components and diverting the inflammatory response.

questions.question: In the pathogenesis of Staphylococcus aureus, coagulase mainly contributes to:
questions.explanation: Coagulase generates a fibrin clot around the bacteria, which promotes abscess formation by isolating the microorganism from the host's immune response.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Staphylococcus aureus protein A is correct?
options: Binds to the Fab fragment of IgA
questions.explanation: Protein A binds to the Fc of IgG, blocking the interaction with Fc receptors on phagocytes, which prevents effective opsonization and phagocytosis of the bacteria.

questions.question: What is the drug of choice for severe MRSA infections?
questions.explanation: Vancomycin is the treatment of choice for severe methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections, due to its efficacy and resistance profile.

questions.question: What alternative antibiotic to vancomycin is used in MRSA orally?
questions.explanation: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone with excellent oral bioavailability, used as an alternative to vancomycin in MRSA infections, especially when oral therapy is required.

questions.question: What is the main adverse effect of vancomycin that requires monitoring?
questions.explanation: Nephrotoxicity is the most relevant adverse effect of vancomycin, especially with high serum levels, so it is recommended to monitor plasma concentrations.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs is active against MRSA but is not used in pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Daptomycin should not be used in pneumonia because it is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant; It is effective in skin and soft tissue infections and MRSA bacteremia.

questions.question: What mechanism of action does linezolid have against SARM?
questions.explanation: Linezolid acts by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 50S subunit of the ribosome, blocking the formation of the initiation complex.

questions.question: What cross-resistance can SARM present against vancomycin?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus can develop intermediate resistance to vancomycin (VISA) due to cell wall thickening, which reduces the effectiveness of the drug.

questions.question: What is the most common anatomical site of carriage of Staphylococcus aureus?
questions.explanation: The anterior nasal passages are the main reservoir of Staphylococcus aureus in healthy individuals, with carriage rates of 20-30% in the general population.

questions.question: What topical antibiotic is used as first line for nasal decolonization due to MRSA?
questions.explanation: Mupirocin 2% nasal ointment is the drug of choice for nasal decolonization of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of mupirocin?
questions.explanation: Mupirocin acts by binding to the bacterial isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase, blocking protein synthesis and exerting a bacteriostatic or bactericidal effect depending on the concentration.

questions.question: What virulence factor of Staphylococcus aureus facilitates its adhesion to the nasal mucosa?
questions.explanation: Protein A, present in the cell wall of Staphylococcus aureus, binds to the Fc domain of immunoglobulins and promotes adhesion to the nasal mucosa, contributing to carriage.

questions.question: What is the recommended duration of topical treatment with mupirocin for nasal decolonization?
questions.explanation: The standard nasal decolonization regimen with 2% mupirocin is 5 days, applying the ointment to both nostrils twice a day.

questions.question: What additional measure is usually combined with mupirocin to reduce MRSA carriage?
questions.explanation: The combination of nasal mupirocin with daily 4% chlorhexidine baths for 5 days is the most used strategy for decolonization of MRSA in colonized patients.

questions.question: What is the main virulence factor involved in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome toxin (TSST-1) is a superantigen produced by Staphylococcus aureus that induces a massive inflammatory response, causing fever, rash and hypotension.

questions.question: What clinical manifestation is characteristic of staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome classically presents with high fever, diffuse scarlet-like rash, and hypotension, due to the action of the TSST-1 toxin as a superantigen.

questions.question: What is the first-line antimicrobial treatment in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome due to MRSA?
questions.explanation: In methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections, vancomycin is the drug of choice for toxic shock syndrome, as it covers resistant strains and is administered intravenously.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains hypotension in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: TSST-1 acts as a superantigen by activating a large number of T lymphocytes, releasing proinflammatory cytokines (cytokine storm) that cause systemic vasodilation and refractory hypotension.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is typical in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Leukopenia is common in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome, along with thrombocytopenia and elevated creatinine, due to the systemic inflammatory response and multiorgan damage.

questions.question: What is the most serious complication of untreated staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
questions.explanation: Acute renal failure is a serious and frequent complication of staphylococcal toxic shock due to sustained hypoperfusion and inflammatory storm, and may require dialysis if it is not reversed in time.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of vegetation in acute S. aureus endocarditis?
questions.explanation: The vegetation in acute S. aureus endocarditis is formed by the aggregation of platelets and fibrin, favored by bacterial adhesion mediated by surface proteins such as clumping factor.

questions.question: Which major Duke criterion is typically met in acute native valve S. aureus endocarditis?
options: Blood culture positive for S. aureus
PCR for bacterial RNA in urine
questions.explanation: The most common Duke major criterion in acute endocarditis due to S. aureus is a positive blood culture for this microorganism, especially if it is persistent or comes from two separate samples.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of acute S. aureus endocarditis in the native valve?
questions.explanation: Septic cerebral embolism is a serious and feared complication in acute S. aureus endocarditis, due to the microorganism's tendency to form friable vegetations that detach and cause septic infarcts.

questions.question: What antibiotic is the treatment of choice for acute endocarditis due to methicillin-sensitive S. aureus in the native valve?
questions.explanation: For methicillin-sensitive S. aureus, intravenous cefazolin is the treatment of choice due to its efficacy, lower nephrotoxicity, and adequate pharmacokinetic profile compared to vancomycin.

questions.question: What is the characteristic echocardiographic finding of acute S. aureus endocarditis in the native valve?
questions.explanation: Mobile and eccentric vegetation, often large and friable, is the typical echocardiographic finding of acute S. aureus endocarditis, reflecting its rapid destructive progression.

questions.question: What mechanism of methicillin resistance does S. aureus present in acute endocarditis?
options: Alteration of the PBP2a target site
questions.explanation: Methicillin resistance in S. aureus is due to the acquisition of the mecA gene, which encodes a penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) with low affinity for beta-lactams, allowing cell wall synthesis.

questions.question: What is the most common etiological agent of hematogenous osteomyelitis in children?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the main pathogen causing acute hematogenous osteomyelitis in children, due to its ability to adhere to bone and form biofilms.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism predominates in septic arthritis due to Staphylococcus aureus?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus produces toxins such as Panton-Valentine leukocidin that directly damage synovial tissue, triggering an acute inflammatory response and joint destruction.

questions.question: What is the empirical antibiotic treatment of choice in acute osteomyelitis due to community MRSA?
questions.explanation: In osteoarticular infections due to methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), vancomycin is the first-line intravenous antibiotic due to its coverage and bone penetration.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic clinical sign of acute septic arthritis in a joint?
questions.explanation: Septic arthritis presents with severe joint pain, extreme limitation of movement and local inflammatory signs, due to the intra-articular purulent response.

questions.question: Which imaging finding suggests acute early-stage osteomyelitis?
questions.explanation: MRI detects bone marrow edema as an early sign of osteomyelitis, before radiological changes appear on plain radiography.

questions.question: What is the most serious complication of untreated septic arthritis due to Staphylococcus aureus?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
questions.explanation: Avascular necrosis, especially of the femoral head, is a devastating complication due to the destruction of articular cartilage and vascular compromise in septic arthritis.
